speaker: interviewer
text: No, that's why I want to get the first part, is-- is your life before.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I didn't start it with drugs.

speaker: interviewer
text: It started with drugs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, 1914.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Well, so that's what we're going to talk about. You-- you were there. You were 10 years old already then. You had--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in my 11th year. I started just going to high school.

speaker: interviewer
text: You've lived in some very turbulent times, that's for sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you need me to voice later or not?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Go ahead and read it to me [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Date is February 9, 1995. We're interviewing Dr. Herbert Zipper. The interviewer is Sandy Jacobson in Los Angeles, USA. We're going to speak English.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Just one second for speed. And now I have speed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dr. Zipper, thank you so much for participating. We're very honored to have you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're very welcome. But you have to excuse my voice. It is not in its natural stage today. I'll try to-- my best to control it, but I can't guarantee.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Would you tell me your full name and when you were born, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My full name is Herbert Zipper. That's all-- that's all of it. I was born April 27, 1904 in the heart of Vienna. You want to have the address?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Number 31 am Graben, that is that large place in the center of the city adjoining to the place in which the casino is, which is the-- the tower that's characterized as Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your family, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father was an inventor, an engineer [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] who represented the spirit of the time of the fin de siecle and of the beginning of this century, which, in a few words, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] meant [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] that every problem of humanity can be solved by science and technology, and that-- that at that time, in the early 1900s, that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the good life could be forever if we just let science and technology work its way, that wars are obsolete, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that security of-- of all could be practically guaranteed. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That means that we young people, we kids, grew up in an atmosphere of utter security, of a security in which everything could be solved. If you just learn enough, do enough scientific research, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] do not [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] be steeped in metaphysical thinking-- that means art became a way of life, in many ways, replaced religion-- religion at-- at the Austrian capital, although it was a Catholic country, and there were, of course, about 180,000 Jews. But religion itself played a very minor role. The middle class was, in many ways, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] living more with the arts than with-- with-- with religion. Excuse me. This was the-- let's say, the climate in which we grew up. I'm not trying to-- to make it sound idealistic. Of course, it was a pipe dream, as you know. And if you read Kafka, you will find out that there were, of course, these thinkers, those who-- the artists, who have the gift-- and I should like to emphasize this-- they do not really live and work beyond their time. Their art sees its own much clearer than the average people. People in general know their past and very little of their presence. It's the artists who really project the presence. That's why they always called-- they are ahead of their time. They aren't ahead of their time. They are with their time. And of course, if you read the great-- the great writers, you will look-- for instance, at the-- at the people who started painting-- if you look at Klimt's painting, if you look at [INAUDIBLE] painting, they were all part of this culture, which was so colorful in retrospect, of course. And I must say, when I was-- when World War I started, I had begun to-- to see this myself. My nose grew faster and bigger. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This was the-- the climate in which we grew up. And I must say, we have been extremely fortunate to grow up in that climate, because learning, I think, was part of-- not of you have to do it, but we wanted to do it, because this was the promise of the good life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me pause just for one second.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So I was asking you about your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother. Well, I'm-- I'm getting a little bit soft when I think of my mother. My mother was the most lovable person. And I know that all my friends, all the friends of my-- my brother and sister, all the people who knew her fell in love with her. She was rosy for everybody. Her name was Regina, but we called her Rosie. My father used to called her Rosie. And her parents called her that way. She was-- she was really, in-- in every way, exuding life, exuding the-- the spirit of life. She-- she was never really-- she never was ill. She was always full of spirit. And I know the parties of the parents that were given at home, which were always visited by our friends-- own friends, young friends. She was always the-- the center of attraction. She was not an intellectual, you know. Of course, she read a great deal. She was a very good pianist. And I mean, she was the daughter of a Viennese middle class. But she helped-- in conversation, she helped making a conversation go as a certain type of Viennese woman could do. You know, [INAUDIBLE] was one of those. I'm not-- I'm not comparing her with her. But [INAUDIBLE] was also not an intellectual. But I have been in one of her Thursday afternoon teas. And the were always some very important people-- this was after the death of Gustav, and her friends. She had the gift of making people-- draw people out of their-- of their shells. And-- and she started strong controversies which were always help for intellectual thinking. My mother could do this, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a bit about the parties that your parents would have, the kind of social life that they had and the people that would come to the house.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't recall much of the parties before the war. I-- I was too young at that time. And we were always sitting, having meals with the parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you're talking about World War I?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. [LAUGHS] Of course, World War I. [INAUDIBLE]. I am that ancient, that's it. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I couldn't recall. I recall that we had-- I recall that both my brother and I, we always listened when-- in the conversation of-- of the adults. This was-- this is what's missing in today's-- today's civilization. The children have their own culture, their own life, while we took part in the life of the adult. And that's where I learned the most. It's-- I-- that is the one thing. I don't recall the persons especially, except, of course, relatives-- especially mother's parents, for instance, especially her father, who was a very strongly intellectual person. They had conversations. And they had, for instance, strong controversies, my father. At that time, my grandfather was a more-- radical is not the right word. He wasn't really a radical. But for my father's point of view, he was more radical than my father. And therefore, there were conversations in which, of course, we both, my brother and I, took delight. And then, World War I started-- these two shots in Sarajevo. I have to say something about Sarajevo, because it is so much in the limelight today, and that somehow characterizes my father, characterizes the whole culture. It was in October, 1908. And I recall my father coming home livid. Overnight, the Austrian government annexed Bosnia and Herzegovina. And my father really furiously said that means only more problems for us. There will be always problems down there. They never can live together, because the one-- the Catholic and the Orthodox and the Islamic people, they never can live together. They somehow-- he used to say the devil has put them there. And he was ranting. And then, of course, he said, that we will have trouble. And that's when the world war started. That's when the shots were fired against the crown president of Austria, Franz Ferdinand and his wife-- that two shots that-- of that-- that radical student, Mr. Princip, who was just-- not a high schooler. I think he was already in college-- who shot both of them. And that started the war, which in retrospect-- and even then-- now, we like to talk about that-- even then, was the most idiotic conflict that ever was. There was really no issue. There was the issue of land grabbing. There was the issue of-- of the-- the weapons industry to make more money on all sides. And if you-- I just gave a lecture last March, last year March. And I read the list of the war declarations of that-- of that 19-- the year 1918. It was funny. It was laughable that every war-- everybody in the world declared war against everybody else, from-- from Central Europe to Western Europe to Eastern Europe to Japan.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your life change after World War I?

speaker: interviewee
text: It changed only during World War I. See, there is one thing which I also observed during World War II when I had my students in Manila, and the young people. When something as cataclysmic happens as a war--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, I think we should-- we have an airplane.

speaker: interviewee
text: Where were we?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We were talking about your life after World War I.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. One grows up twice as fast in such times of real trouble. You see, the security that we felt so strongly was suddenly broken. We didn't know, is-- has Father to go to war? Or is he going to stay home? He was-- at that time, he was technical director of the-- possibly, I think it was the last largest central European industrial combine. He had to-- under his leadership, there were 52 plants all over Austria. And one-- one third of the year, he was always out traveling. So he couldn't-- they-- they wouldn't let him go to war. But he didn't know, because one day, he had appeared in uniform.

speaker: interviewer
text: He appeared in uniform?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And so therefore, our interest into-- in-- in what is happening, that was so strongly focused to our own life, to the life at home, was suddenly projected entirely to the world. That means we grew up much faster. A boy, in-- let's say, in the winter of 1914, '15, could not be compared to a boy of the same age today because we were living with-- with the history that was being made. And in addition to that, in the summer when-- when before the war was really started, when the diplomatic stupidities were being enacted, there was a young poet who accompanied us-- our governess and all we children-- when we were-- when we walked every morning to Schonbrunn. We didn't go to the country because my father was afraid of-- of the war coming up, that we should be at home.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was a-- he was a real radical-- a poet who saw his time and who explained to me in no uncertain terms what the powers are, what really drives [INAUDIBLE]. And he was really right. My father prohibited my governess that-- that any contact with that man-- when he heard me talking about these things, he said, who is that who is talking? You see?

speaker: interviewee
text: But my governess, seemingly, who was a lovely person, seemingly was of the same opinion. And so I got a very good education about-- about our government, about the French government, about Russia. He was a very well-educated young man. I think he was-- he was imprisoned.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you're talking about the poet?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But I owe him a great deal of early waking up to realities and not to the fairy tales that society is spreading, especially through the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you-- how were you educated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where I was educated like other one, I went to-- to the gymnasium, to the Latin school and had eight years of Latin and five years of Greek. And-- and I got what-- what was called the education of a-- of a well-- well-rounded middle class boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you went to-- did you-- you had tutoring at-- at home until you were how old?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I left first grade only. But I had that governess. She was-- she taught us French. And I was pretty fluent. We talked with her only in French.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what about when you started studying music? When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was long before the war I started. My mother had a-- a piano teacher. I got a piano teacher of my own first in Italian by the name of Radovanni. And he lasted only a few months, because I didn't like him. I said he was impossible.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I wanted the piano teacher of my mother. What was her name? Well, it's not-- that's immaterial. It may come after. It comes to me-- Mrs. Wukowitch-- not so easy to remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was very good. And-- and I was playing the piano pretty soon in a way so that I could play with my mother, four-handed piano, and [? perform ?] Schubert and Haydn pieces so that we could make music together. Music was part of life. I mean, you-- I cannot tell you what it was, because today, you have that eternal loudspeaker that is accompanying our life every day all the-- all the 24 hours. You cannot even listen to a stupid advertising without having something in the background going on, groaning. The other day, I heard something that was too funny for words-- a-- a bass tuba accompanying an automobile sales pitch with-- with groaning-- completely senseless noises. But that was called music.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did you realize that music, more than being just a part of your life as you recognized it, would be how you would-- what-- what your occupation would be?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, it was not such an extremely outrageous idea at that time. Being a musician was part of society. And I can tell you, if-- if a kid went into the street car with a violin case, everybody looked approvingly at that kid-- not today. I don't know in Vienna. I haven't seen any kids with violins, kids going to the street car. But that was part of it. You see, I grew up without any of the electronic medias. There was no radio, no television, no gramophone recording, nothing. If you wanted to have music, you have to make it yourself or you had to go to a concert or possibly to a church. That was all.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had some famous musicians that were part of your-- they were part of your circle, or you would meet with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I know, sure. I mean, they-- then when later on, when I, of course, studied seriously. I studied seriously immediately, actually. And I recorded a conversation with my father when I was about 10 or 11-- 11. It was during the war. Maybe it was 12. No, that is not-- it's rather immaterial. I-- he said to me, Herbert, it seems to me that you want to be taking music as a profession. And I said, you are guessing right. I just want to tell you that-- I don't want to talk you out of it. I just want to tell you what the problems are, I mean, what the financial problems are, what the social problems are, what's-- what's in store, and how hard it is-- that music-- the study of music is much more intricate than any other study. And I learned at that the time, clearly, what he-- he said that. It was very-- very observing. He said, if you learn in high school your mathematics, your algebra, your-- your geometry, and so forth, you can go to the technical high school, to the technical college, and in four years, you'll get a diploma that you're a good engineer-- that you can do. You cannot go and do all these things that you need to learn as an-- as an educated person and then go to college and say, I want to learn the violin. You have to start as you started, he said, when you are a very small kid. You have to grow up with the music. You have to become-- music, as he-- he knew it-- music is a much more complicated and a much more demanding thing than any other of the things. Maybe, he said, maybe medicine may be that difficult.

speaker: interviewee
text: So you have to face a very hard study in addition to what you do with everybody else-- I mean your Latin, your algebra, [INAUDIBLE]. So he didn't scare me. But he felt it was important to tell me. And I'm not-- when I decided, yes, then I had his support. His and mother's support were there at all times.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had no problem with my family being-- being a musician. That problem came later on because of the question of space, because what-- what after-- what Vienna was after the war, after we lost the war, can-- cannot be imagined, how a city becomes devoid of any amenities. There was absolutely no food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you're talking again about World War I?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no food. There was nothing to heat with. Most of the time, we didn't get electricity because there was no-- no way, no coal that they could fire the plants-- the plants. It was a really desperate time. The reason why I'm still getting up

speaker: interviewee
text: 00 in the morning, at 4:30-- it's what I-- that's what I had to do. Before school, I went around to every market in the neighborhood with my bicycle that had leather-- leather pneumatics--

speaker: interviewer
text: Wheels, tires.

speaker: interviewee
text: Tires-- leather tires, because rubber was-- was unavailable-- just to find out where I could get some of these ugly beets and things that you-- that you just fill your stomach with. Let's say my winter coat, at that time-- I grew-- at that time, in 1918, I was full-- my full size. I was 6 foot 1 and 1/2. Now, I've shrunk like Irish linen. But I was really very tall, grew out of all my things. So my winter coat was made out of paper. You have a very-- it is impossible to imagine for any American what a city becomes when it loses, suddenly, every raison d'etre, practically. This was the large capital of a-- of a large empire. And suddenly, this empire was gone. And it was a small country of 7 million people who all tried to eke out a life for themselves. It was a very bad time, also, socially. People didn't trust each other. Each one-- everybody start-- started to-- to look out for himself only, to try to grab something to eke out just a sheer livelihood. The question of starvation is one of the most demeaning questions-- when suddenly, everything else goes into the background, and you cannot get rid of the idea-- food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you notice, at that time, a rise in anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time-- I don't recall a time without anti-Semitism in any form or another. This is an endemic disease-- endemic disease that has accompanied the lives of the Europeans, and many other countries too, for the past 2,000 years. I mean, one has to-- it was not-- of course, it became worse, because they-- there was this part of-- of so-called intellectual Europe that started to blame the Jews for the war. When you have to find-- there was a comic-- a German comic in Munich who said about the anti-Semitism, it's-- it's-- it's the Jews and the bicyclists who are to be blamed. You know. And-- and he was asked-- this was on stage. He was asked why-- because there are too many of them anywhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your--

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE].
